Commissioner of Agriculture, Maharashtra State, Pune-411 001 Foreword The project on pest surveillance was launched during year 2011-12 by commissionerate of Agriculture (Horticulture), Government of Maharashtra, Pune. The mega project has major objective to reduce the time lag between occurrence of the pests and application of need based pest management interventions to restrict the damage due to the pests in targeted fruit crops so as to minimize environmental hazards and adverse effect on human health and beneficial fauna. The objectives of the project are met by recording pest activities of major horticultural crops in structured formats, uploading the data on pest activities to centralized server and issuance of timely IPM advisories. The project implementation is facilitated through linkage between state agricultural universities (Mahatma Phule Krishi Vidyapeeth, Rahuri, Dr. Balasaheb Sawant Konkan Krishi Vidyapeeth, Dapoli, Dr. Panjabrao Deshmukh Krishi Vidyapeeth, Akola, Vasantrao Naik Marathwada Krishi Vidyapeeth, Parabhani, Banana Research Station, Jalgaon and ICAR institutes (National Research Centre for Integrated Pest Management, New Delhi, Central Citrus Research Institute, Nagpur, National Research Centre on Pomegranate, Solapur and National Research Centre for Banana, Tiruchirapalli,) and state department of Horticulture, Government of Maharashtra, Pune. In order to streamline the working of the project, the information on pests and diseases of banana, mango and pomegranate and pest surveillance was compiled in year 2013 to educate and enabled the field functionaries to identify the important pests and diseases and their symptoms of damage as well as methodologies to record pest data in structured format . data sheet and upload such data in to the centralized server. It also facilitated the project scientists’ easy accessibility of such data and department of Horticulture personnel to make available short-term & detailed IPM advisories on the internet and transmit them through SMS for benefits of farmers and other end users. As result of addition of few other fruit crops like sweet orange, Nagpur mandarin and sapota in the pest surveillance programme from the year 2014-15, need was (cid:88)(cid:88)(cid:88)

Pests of Fruit Trees (Citrus, Banana, Mango, Pomegranate and Sapota) felt to rewrite the present book to provide information on the additional crops and update and revise the information on pests and IPM technologies for the existing crops. Not only few fruit crops have been added but area of operation of the project has also been widened. Earlier area of operation was restricted to districts of the state, but now 14 more districts have been added making the total tally 23 covering ,61,647 ha, amounting to 95.86% increase in area of operation. With the constant and untiring efforts of the project scientists and field functionaries of the Department of Horticulture, we could successfully fulfill the objectives of the project and implement its activities. It is visible from the rising trend in productivity of mango, pomegranate and banana in year 2013-14 over years 20012-13 and 2011-12. Updated book on pest surveillance and information on pests and management advisories shall be able to provide specific skill to field functionaries and educate them as well as progressive farmers and other users. For this complement the project scientists and the staff of department of Horticulture, Government of Maharashtra, Pune for their dedicated efforts and wish them all success for future endeavours in this regard. hope the outcome of the project shall be able to stand the test of time. (Vikas Deshmukh) (cid:88)(cid:101)

NCIPM Introduction Global transformation in the food system, change in the consumption pattern away from the rice, wheat and pulses and health consciousness are causing major shift in the focus towards the horticultural crops. This increasing recognition of the horticultural produce for nutritional values in human diet has resulted greater demand for fruits in the market. Innovations in production technology of horticultural crops and acceptance of these as mean of diversification have contributed significantly in meeting the increased demand of these crops. Hence India with . per cent global land mass and 16 per cent global population also recognized the importance of horticultural crops as engine empowering not only nutritional security but also as national food security. Fruit production of India is 88.98 million tonnes from an area of .21 million hectares that is rated second in the world after China. Not only it rank in total fruit production but also it enjoy privilege of growing large variety of fruits, of which mango, banana, citrus, pomegranate, guava, grape, pineapple, papaya, apple, sapota and litchi are the major ones. Fruits are grown throughout the country, but the state of Maharashtra is contributing significantly to the extent of 15.12 per cent toward fruit basket of India followed by AP (11.81), Gujarat (.99), TN (.28), UP (.74), Karnataka (.47), MP (.40), Telangana (.99), Bihar (.51). It is not only major fruit growing state of India but also growing large variety of fruits like mango, grapes, citrus, sapota, pomegranate, banana and papaya. Mango is the most important fruit covering 30.10 per cent of the area and accounting for .01 per cent of the total fruit production of the state followed by citrus (sweet orange, Nagpur mandarin, lime) (17.57 % area and 13.09% production), grapes (.75% and 16.05%), pomegranate (.70% and .02%), banana (.30 % and 35.90 %) and sapota (.66% and .53%), respectively. Mango, grapes and pomegranate are the three important fruit crops exported to many countries which follows very stringent quality measures. Having recognized the importance of cultivation of fruits in the state for greater role in providing the livelihood security to the local farmers and nutritional security at the national level, the state department of Horticulture started paying greater attention toward quality standard as well as in preventing losses due to the pests of fruits. There are many other programmes running in the state in meeting these listed objectives, but ICT based -pest surveillance programme was started in year 2011-12 to keep constant watch on the pests and diseases activities and issuance of timely ecofriendly integrated pest management advisories to prevent losses due to the pests so as to minimize the contamination of the produce by pesticides residues and looking after important environment and quarantine issues. Since then the project continued to meet the its targets and the objectives successively in its fourth year (2014-15) which are (cid:32)

Pests of Fruit Trees (Citrus, Banana, Mango, Pomegranate and Sapota) reflected in the rising trend shown in the productivity of targeted fruit crops like mango, banana and pomegranate. The productivity (MT/HA) of mango, banana and pomegranate was ., 58. and 10. in year 2013-14 which is higher over year 2012-13 (., 43., .) and 2011-12 (., 52., .), respectively. It was made possible through the constant efforts of the state department of Horticulture, scientists of state agricultural university and ICAR institutes and through establishment of strong linkage among themselves, the stake holders and the end uses of the technologies and by reducing pest damage and other constraints of increasing the production and productivity. Looking to successful implementation of the pest surveillance, three other fruit crops like sweet orange, Nagpur mandarin and sapota were also included in the project for timely application of integrated management programme against pests and diseases from the year 2014-15. This necessitated the need for development, up gradation and modification of the software and revision of the -pest surveillance manual which is presented in the following pages. It is sincerely hoped that the present compilation shall cater the need of the field functionaries and progressive farmers in creating awareness for the correct identification of the pests, timely and ETL based application of the IPM technologies without time lag between the occurrence of the pests and their management. It will also facilitate the better implementation of the project activities. (cid:33)

Bhede Major insect pests and diseases of citrus and their management Major Insect Pests Citrus leaf miner (Phyllocnistis citrella) Symptoms (cid:120) The characteristic symptom of leaf miner is presence of silvery serpentine mines usually on the under surface of the leaf. In general, each leaf has only single mine, but in case of heavy infestation there may be several mines per leaf. (cid:120) Mining of the leaves causes them to curl up, distorted and thereby reducing the photosynthetic area of the young foliage. (cid:120) In case of severe infestation, mines can also be seen on the upper side of the leaves as well as on shoot Citrus leaf miner portion of new twigs. (cid:120) Damage by this pest predisposes the acid lime plants for development of canker disease. Pest identification (cid:120) Eggs: Eggs are laid singly usually on the underside of leaves near the midrib and look like tiny water droplets. (cid:120) Larva: The full grown larva measures . mm in length and is pale yellow or pale green with light-brown well developed mandibles. They settle down in galleries near the leaf margin for pupation. (cid:34)

Pests of Fruit Trees (Citrus, Banana, Mango, Pomegranate and Sapota) (cid:120) Adult: Adult is tiny silvery-white moth about mm long with fringed wings. Fore wings have brown stripes and prominent black spot near the apical margin while hind wings are pure white with wing spread of -5mm. Biology of the pest The eggs hatch within - days. The young pale yellow larvae immediately start feeding between epidermal layers of the leaf. They pupate, when full grown, near the margin of the mined leaf. The total life cycle is about - weeks. Management practices (cid:120) Avoid pruning during active growth periods as it may induce further new flush and thereby allow the pest to have more number of generations. If necessary, prune only the infested shoots during winter from the inner canopy. (cid:120) Remove the water shoots from the tree canopy as they are the preferred sites of attack. (cid:120) The spray timings are most important in managing leaf miner because before entering in leaf tissue it is highly susceptible to the toxic effect of pesticides. Foliar application of neem oil ml or imidacloprid17. SL @. ml or phosolone 35 EC @. ml or fenvalerate 20 EC @. ml or spinosad 45 SC @ .34 ml or novaluron 10 EC @ .87 ml/ of water or mak all season horticultural mineral oil @ .% in flushing season by directing at the new flush checks the pest. (cid:120) Conserve the bio-agents (Coccinellids, Chrysopids predators and eulophid parasitoids) by avoiding application of insecticides during late winter to early spring. Citrus psylla (Diaphorina citri) Symptoms (cid:120) The damage is caused by both nymphs and adults by sucking the cell sap from the leaves, tender shoots and flowers causing curling of leaves, defoliation and drying of twigs. (cid:120) Nymphs secrete whitish crystalline honey dew which attracts the growth of fungus, adversely affecting the Citrus psylla on leaves (cid:35)

NCIPM photosynthesis. Late instar nymphs and adults also transmit the citrus greening disease. (cid:120) In case of severe attack the leaf buds, flower buds and leaves may wilt and die. Pest identification (cid:120) Eggs: The eggs are bright yellow and deposited on unopened leaf buds. (cid:120) Nymph: Early instar nymphs are green or dull orange and late instar nymphs are bright yellowish orange. (cid:120) Adult: It is - mm long, mottled brown in color, with transparent wings. Biology of the pest Eggs are laid on tips of growing shoots and between unfurling leaves. Females lay more than 800 eggs during their lives. Nymphs pass through five instars. Total life cycle requires 15 to 47 days depending upon the season. Adults may live for several months. There is no diapause, but population are low in peak Winter and Summer seasons . There are nine to ten generations year; however, maximum of 16 have been reported. Management practices (cid:120) Management of psylla during Ambia (March-April) and Mrig (June-July) flush is must as the pest causes severe damage. (cid:120) Collateral host like curry leaf (Murraya koenigii Linn.) plant should not be grown in the vicinity of citrus orchards as it is the most preferred host plant of psylla. (cid:120) Foliar application of quinalphos 25 EC @ ml or novaluron 10 EC @ .55 ml or thiamethoxam 25WG @.32g/ of water at bud burst stage. If required, second spray of any of the above insecticides should be given after 15 days. (cid:120) Conserve the bio-agents (Coccinellids, Chrysopids, Eulophids) by avoiding application of insecticides from winter to early spring. (cid:120) Two releases of Chrysopid predator, Mallada desjardensi @ 30 larvae/tree in each flushing season reduces the citrus psylla incidence. Citrus blackfly (Aleurocanthus woglumi) Symptoms (cid:120) Both adults and nymphs suck plant sap and results in the curling of leaves and also the premature fall of flower buds and the developing fruits thereby resulting in reducing the vitality of the tree. (cid:36)

Pests of Fruit Trees (Citrus, Banana, Mango, Pomegranate and Sapota) (cid:120) Moreover, honey dew secretion favours rapid development of black sooty mould that covers entire plant surface. (cid:120) The process of photosynthesis is hampered greatly resulting in stunted growth of plants, Citrus blackfly on leaves low intensity of flowering and scarce fruiting. Pest identification (cid:120) Egg: Yellowish brown oval and lay in whorls of lower side of the leaves. (cid:120) Nymphs: The nymphs are scale like, shiny black and spiny with marginal fringes. (cid:120) Pseudo-pupa: Oval, black in colour and its dorsum is arched with long black spines, the margins have rounded black teeth. (cid:120) Adult: Adults are ashy coloured with brick red abdomen, smoky wings. The adult females are about . mm long and the males are . mm in length. Biology of the pest The adults emerge in flushing seasons and the females lay yellowish brown oval shaped eggs which are arranged in spiral manner on underside of leaves with 15-22 eggs in cluster. The eggs hatch in -14 days and the nymphs on emergence start feeding on cell sap and settle on the lower side of the leaf. There are two distinct broods in year. The first brood adults emerge in March-April and those of the second brood emerge in July-October. Management practices (cid:120) Close planting, water logging should be avoided. (cid:120) Excessive irrigation and nitrogen fertilization should be avoided. (cid:120) Avoid growing collateral hosts of the pest, guava, sapota and pomegranate. (cid:120) Foliar application of imidacloprid 17. SL @ . ml or acephate 75 WP @ .25 or phosalone 35 EC @. ml or dimethoate 30 EC@ ml or novaluron 10 EC @ .79 ml/ of water on the lower sides of leaves covering the entire tree canopy at 50% egg hatching stage. Second spray should be given after 15 days with any of the above insecticides or neem oil @10 ml/ of water. (cid:37)

NCIPM Citrus whitefly (Dialeurodes citri) Symptoms (cid:120) Both nymphs and adults suck the plant sap and secrete honeydew due to which sooty mould develops on the leaves. (cid:120) Severe infestation results in black fungal layer manifestation, covering entire plant parts including fruits due to which photosynthesis is affected. Pest identification Citrus whitefly on leaves (cid:120) Egg: Oval, pale yellow and rest on small stalks and singly on the underside of soft young leaves. (cid:120) Nymph: The nymphs are oval in shape, scale like, blackish with marginal bristle like fringes and are stationary. (cid:120) Pseudo-pupa: Oval, pale yellow, with an orange or yellow band in the middle of the body. (cid:120) Adult: Adult is . mm long with white or greyish wings, pale yellow body and red constricted eyes. Biology of the pest (cid:120) The adult female lays about 150 eggs on the lower surface of leaves which hatches in about 10 days. The crawlers settle on the under surface of the leaves and suck the sap. Nymphal life averages 23 to 30 days. Pseudo pupal development requires 13 to 30 days. The adult lives an average of about 10 days, but has been known to live for as long as 27 days. The entire life cycle from egg to adult requires from 41 to 333 days. Management practices (cid:120) Same as given for blackfly. Citrus aphid (Toxoptera aurantii, Myzus persicae Sulzer, Aphis spp.) Symptoms (cid:120) Nymphs and adults suck sap from tender leaves and shoots during winter to early spring. Affected leaves turn yellow, curled, deformed and dry up. Growth of young shoots is adversely affected. Plant growth is stunted. (cid:120) Sooty mould is produced on honeydew excreted by aphids. (cid:38)

Pests of Fruit Trees (Citrus, Banana, Mango, Pomegranate and Sapota) (cid:120) Aphids also act as the active vectors of Citrus Tristeza Virus. Pest identification (cid:120) Nymph: The nymphs are dark reddish brown and approximately less than . mm in length. (cid:120) Adult: Adults are soft-bodied, pear-shaped, tiny sucking insects, measuring less than mm in length and are pale yellowish green to black in colour. They have pair of cornicles arising from vth abdominal Citrus aphid segment. Biology of the pest (cid:120) Each aphid produces about young nymphs/day for period of - weeks parthenogenetically. Single life-cycle normally takes to days. Management practices (cid:120) Foliar application of dimethoate 30 EC @ ml or acephate 75 WP @ or imidacloprid 17. SL @ . ml / on identified infested trees during winter season reduces the infestation levels. If needed, Second and third sprays should follow at 10 days intervals. Fruit sucking moth (Eudocima materna) Symptoms (cid:120) Adult moths are active during dusk and suck the juice from the ripening yellow colour fruits during Sept.-Nov. (cid:120) The punctures produced during feeding by the adult moths leads to fruit rot due to invasion by secondary pathogens. (cid:120) Only adult moths are destructive to citrus Fruit sucking moth fruits. The moths are distinguished by having particularly well developed proboscis with dentate tips with which they are able to pierce the ripening fruits. Pest identification (cid:120) Egg: Round, translucent, measuring about mm in diameter. (cid:120) Larva: Full grown caterpillars are 50 - 60 mm long, stout, velvety-blue with yellow colour. (cid:39)

NCIPM (cid:120) Adult: The adults have pale orange brown body with forewings dark greyish and the hind wings orange red with two black curved patches. Biology of the pest (cid:120) The female moth lays eggs on weeds like Tinospora cardifolia, . semilacina, Cocculus hirsutus, Convolvulus arvensis etc. where the caterpillar develops after hatching. Egg period is - 10 days. The caterpillar is semilooper, dark brown with yellow and red spots. Full grown caterpillars are 50 - 60 mm long, stout, velvety-blue with yellow patterns on dorsal and lateral sides. Larval period is 28 - 35 days. Pupation takes place in transparent pale whitish silken cover enclosed in leaf fold. Pupal period is 14 - 18 days. Management practices (cid:120) Destroy fallen fruits by burrying in the ground. (cid:120) Clean cultivation of the orchard is must to avoid the pest development. (cid:120) Generation of smoke in the late evening hours in orchards repels the pest. (cid:120) Systematic destruction of larval host plants during rainy season in the vicinity and surrounding the orchards in mass campaign mode. (cid:120) Bagging of fruits is effective but very laborious and expensive. (cid:120) Poison baiting with malathion 50 EC @ 10 ml + 100 jaggary +100 ml mandarin juice + 900 ml of water (two bottles containing poison bait per 25-30 trees). (cid:120) Foliar application of neem oil % or malathion 50 EC @ ml at 10 -15 days interval during fruit maturity till harvest reduces the infestation levels. Bark eating caterpillar (Indarbela quadrinotata) Symptoms (cid:120) Caterpillars feed on the bark and bore at stem joints Larva remains hidden inside the tunnel during day time and becomes active in the night. (cid:120) Several caterpillars may attack the same tree at different locations which can cause serious injury to the bark and the death of small branches. (cid:120) The holes left on the trunk may lead to infestation by other insects or plant pathogens. Affected branches break at the points of attack. Bark eating caterpillar (cid:120) severe infestation may arrest the growth of the tree and die within - years if untreated . (cid:40)

Pests of Fruit Trees (Citrus, Banana, Mango, Pomegranate and Sapota) Pest identification (cid:120) Eggs: Female lays eggs in group of 15 to 25 during May-June which remain attached to bark. (cid:120) Larva: Caterpillars are 50-60 mm and have pale brown bodies with dark brown heads. (cid:120) Adult: Adults are pale brown or grey in colour. They are 35 - 40 mm in size. The fore wings are pale rufous with numerous dark rufous bands. Their hind wings are fuscous. Biology of the pest (cid:120) The eggs hatch in -10 days. The larvae have the habit of making webs along the feeding galleries. The larvae take as many as -11 months to complete development. When full-grown, they make hole into the wood and pupate inside. The pupal stage lasts - weeks. The moths emerge with the onset of monsoon and are short lived. Only one generation is completed in year. Management practices . Clear the affected branches of the fross and faeces. ii. Inject -10 ml of dichlorvos 76 EC @ ml/ into the tunnel and cover with cotton wad is quite effective due to its contact and fumigant action. Other Pest Citrus rust mite (Phyllocoptruta oleivora Ashmead) Symptoms (cid:120) Citrus mite attack is known as ‘Rusting’ on Grapefruit, ‘Lalya’ on Nagpur mandarin, Mangu on Sathdudi sweet orange and rind disorder in Kinnow. (cid:120) Rust mite infestation produces multitude of brown–reddish black spots on the fruit surface. (cid:120) The rust mite attacks the berry size fruits of Citrus rust mite symptom on fruit both Ambia in March-April and Mrig in October- November inflicting undesirable brown irregular patches on them that fetches low market price to the fruits. Pest identification (cid:120) Eggs: The eggs are smooth, spherical, semi-translucent and are laid in groups in indentations on fruits and on ventral surfaces of leaves. (cid:32)(cid:31)

NCIPM (cid:120) Larva: The newly hatched larva resembles the adult, changing in color from clear to lemon yellow. (cid:120) Adult: adults are very small (150 -165 μm), fusiform, dorsally flattened and yellow. Biology of the pest Egg period is days and nymphal development completes in -11 days. The life cycle is completed in -10 days in summer and 14 days in winter. female lays 20 to 30 eggs during life span of 20 days. The pest has number of generations. Management practices (cid:120) Water stress often aggravates mite problem. Make sure that trees are well irrigated, particularly during the stress in peak summer and winter. (cid:120) Foliar application of wettable sulfur 85 DP @ or dicofol 18. EC @ ml or propargite 57 EC @ 1ml or ethion 50 EC @ ml/ of water and make all season HMO (%) immediately after fruit set and during active period of mites checks the pest effectively. Major Diseases Root rot, foot rot and gummosis of citrus (Phytophthora spp.) Symptoms (cid:120) Root rot occurs when the cortex of fibrous is infected trunks soft and appears water-soaked, fibrous roots slough of their cortex, learing only white thread- like stele. (cid:120) Gummosis is identified by profuse gumming on the surface of the bark on tree trunk from which gummy substance oozes out. (cid:120) The affected bark turns dark brown and develops longitudinal cracks. The wood tissues are also Root rot, foot rot and affected. In severe cases, bark rots and the trees dry gummosis of citrus because of girdling effect. (cid:120) The disease is favoured by rains, heavy to ill drained soils, excessive and flood method of irrigation leading to prolonged contact of trunk and crown with water or moist soil, high water-table, deep planting, low budding and injuries to roots or base of the stem. (cid:32)(cid:32)

Pests of Fruit Trees (Citrus, Banana, Mango, Pomegranate and Sapota) Pathogen dissemnation (cid:120) The primary means by which Phytophthora spp. are spread through citrus orchards is by use of infested nursery stock. The pathogen may be present in soil or infected roots even though disease symptoms are not readily apparent. The fungus -like organism is also carried in soil on equipment when vehicles move from infested to non-infested groves or nurseries. Flooding method for irrigation water may also move the pathogen from area to area. Within groves, dispersal by irrigation water occurs especially where furrow or flood irrigation is used. Surface water following heavy rains may carry the pathogens it drains from the grove. Management practices (cid:120) Trunk Paint: Scrap the affected bark portions of the tree trunk, branches and limbs along with some healthy green bark with the help of sharp Knife or Khurpa. Properly collect and destroy the diseased bark by burning and do not allow it to fall on the ground. Apply the fungicidal paint of mefenoxam MZ (available as Ridomil Gold)with the help of painting brush twice in year whenever symptoms appear. (cid:120) Soil Drench: Drenching the root zone area of the infected tree with Mefenoxam MZ (25 / tree in 10 litres of water) twice in February-March and July-August. Trunk paint can be combined with soil drench for effective disease control. (cid:120) Foliar Sprays: Spray application of fosetyl aluminium (Aliette)(. /litre) can be made during September-October for the effective disease control. The spray application will check the lesions on the tree parts as well as help regeneration of feeder roots. Aliette spray can be combined with drenching of root zone area with mefenoxam MZ. (cid:120) Prefer double ring basin method of irrigation over flooding method of irrigation. Citrus canker (Xanthomonas citri subsp. citri) Symptoms (cid:120) Canker lesions start as pinpoint spots and attain diameter of -10 mm. (cid:120) Their eventual size depends mainly on the cultivar and the age of the host tissue at infection. (cid:32)(cid:33)

NCIPM (cid:120) The lesions are initially circular but later may develop irregularly often aggregated at the leaf margin or the leaf tips or in restricted area of the leaf. Lesion become visible on the underside of leaves about days after infection and on the upper surface soon after. (cid:120) The characteristic symptom of the disease on leaves is the yellow halo that surrounds lesion. Young lesions are raised or pustular, particularly on the lower leaf surface. (cid:120) The pustules later became corky and crateriform, with raised margin and sunken centre. Lesions on fruit and stems extend - mm in depth and are superficially similar to those on leaves. Citrus canker Pathogen dissemination () on leaf, () on fruit (cid:120) With strong winds, high rain-fall, and temperature between 200 and 300 , the citrus canker disease becomes very much severe in concerned area. Strong winds and rain splashes greatly help disseminating the bacteria, while the required temperature and moisture help successful infection. Management practices (cid:120) Eradication of the pathogen inoculum is the best method of controlling the disease. All infected and suspected trees should be burnt and workers tools and cloths should be disinfected. Where the eradication of the pathogen by burning diseased trees is not feasible, the following recommendations should be adopted : () Use of disease-free nursery stock for planting in new orchards, () Pruning of the affected twigs in old orchards and spraying with fungicides like Bordeaux mixture (.%) or, copper oxychloride (.%) at periodical intervals particularly during rainy season, and () Bordeaux mixture (.%) spray on plants before they are planted in new orchards. (cid:120) Strict quarantine measures should be followed in canker-free citrus producing areas so that the pathogen be not allowed to that area. (cid:120) Use of resistant varieties should be preferred. Although no resistant varieties to this disease has been successfully developed, varieties of mandarin oranges are resistant to canker, compared to lime/lemon groups. (cid:32)(cid:34)

Pests of Fruit Trees (Citrus, Banana, Mango, Pomegranate and Sapota) Greening disease ( Candidatus liberibacter asiaticus) Symptoms (cid:120) The range and severity of symptoms vary with season, type and extent of infection, age and nutritional status of the trees. (cid:120) Greening infected citrus leaves are generally small, upright and frequently have symptoms with green veins and chlorotic interveinal areas. (cid:120) Diseased leaves also show various types of chlorotic mottling. Leaf mottle is one of the best diagnostic symptoms of greening. (cid:120) In severe cases leaves become almost chlorotic with scattered dark green islands. (cid:120) Symptoms are often seen on part of the canopy or Greening disease even only in branch or twig. () on leaves, () on fruit (cid:120) Infected fruits are small and mis-shapen, many fall prematurely, while those that remain on the tree do not colour properly, remaining green on the stylar (lower) end and hence the origin of the name ‘greening’. Pathogen dissemination (cid:120) Greening disease is transmitted by infected budwoods and in the orchards through citrus psylla (Diaphorina citri). Psylla picks the pathogen in nymphal stage and transmits it when adult. Management practices (cid:120) The management of greening disease involves removal of affected unproductive trees and their replacement by disease-free budded plants developed on improved rootstock through proper indexing programme. (cid:120) Regulatory (quarantine) measures should be strengthened to limit movement, sale and use of infected bud wood or nursery stock. Strict control of nurseries through registered disease-free certification scheme is essential to prevent the spread of disease. (cid:120) Since the disease also spreads through the vector, citrus psylla, recommended insecticides as given under psylla management should be applied to control the disease spread. (cid:32)(cid:35)

NCIPM Twig blight/twig dieback (Botryodiplodia theobromae, Colletotrichum gloeosporioides) Symptoms (cid:120) Twig blight is common problem of citrus especially mandarin plantation in India. Nagpur mandarin plants in central India show drying of twigs after one or two bearings. (cid:120) number of stress factors like nutritional deficiencies, drought, attack of insect pests, virus and virus-like diseases and root rot infection by Phytophthora spp. together Twig blight/twig dieback contribute to the problem. (cid:120) The plants affected with one or more of the above factors show drying of twigs starting from the tip and die back. (cid:120) Reduction of canopy volume due to this malady reduces the yield considerably. Blighted trees on bearing suffer heavy fruit drop. The fruit harvested from such trees develop high percentage of stem end rot during storage. Pathogen dissemination (cid:120) Although pycnidia are commonly present in dead bark and wood, the fungus is usually considered primary invader after tissues are wounded by pruning, mechanical damage, or unfav orable environmental conditions, such as extreme heat, cold, flooding or drought. (cid:120) The primary cause of dieback is probably physiological weakness that renders the tissues more prone to colonization by this weak pathogen. Rain splash also help pathogen spread from one tree to another. Management practices (cid:120) The best management strategy of this problem is to remove the predisposing factors responsible for weakening the plant vigour. (cid:120) Regular pruning of dead twigs - cm below the dead portion after harvest and spray of benzimidazole fungicides (.. carbendazim @ .%) twice at monthly interval after pruning keep the problem under control. (cid:32)(cid:36)

Pests of Fruit Trees (Citrus, Banana, Mango, Pomegranate and Sapota) Scab (Elsinoe fawcettii) Symptoms (cid:120) Citrus scab attacks the fruit, leaves and twigs, producing slightly raised, irregular scabby or wart-like outgrowths. (cid:120) The scabs are grey or pinkish at first and become darker with age. They are more common on fruits than leaves. (cid:120) The raised lumps associated with scab can Scab caused on fruits be confused with symptoms caused by citrus canker or with windrub abrasions. (cid:120) Spores of the fungus are readily produced on the surface of scab lesions on young fruits and leaves throughout the year. Pathogen dissemination (cid:120) Spores of the fungus are spread in the orchard by rain, overhead irrigation and during spraying operations. Dew may also cause the spores to be liberated from the lesions but due to the limited splashing action, there would only be localised dispersal. Management practices (cid:120) Prophylactic sprays of captafol, benomyl or methyl thiophanate at emergence of new flush provide satisfactory control. (cid:120) To control citrus scab, spray the tree with Copper oxychloride, Captan could also be applied in the late winter before the new spring growth starts and second spray application should be applied shortly after the petals have dropped from the fruit blossoms. (cid:120) To help reduce the spread and growth of citrus scab never use water sprinkler on citrus tree. When watering citrus tree, always use soaker (or, drip) hose on the ground near the tree’ drip line. General management practices (cid:120) As precautionary measure first spray may be given as soon as the new flush is emerged. (cid:120) Destroy the ant colonies in the orchards as they are the carriers of certain pests to their feeding sites and also protect them from other harmful agencies. (cid:32)(cid:37)

NCIPM (cid:120) Close spacing and water logging conditions should be avoided in the orchards which help in creating micro-niche favouring the pest population. (cid:120) Avoid pruning during active growth periods as it induces irregular and frequent flushes which lead to the perpetuation of pest. If necessary, prune only the infested dry shoots after fruit harvest. (cid:120) Apply nitrogenous fertilizers as per need only as excessive and frequent applications promote new flushes which provide favourable conditions for insect pests’ infestation. (cid:120) Modify canopy structure in such way that light interception is maximum below the canopy. (cid:120) Preparations of spray solution, spraying operations, insecticide residue and compatibility in mixtures are important aspects to keep in mind before undertaking the sprays. (cid:120) The time of insecticide application should be decided after monitoring the pest incidence meticulously viz., only young and vulnerable life stages of the pest should be sprayed upon. (cid:120) The spray should be targeted on the lower surface of the leaves and the new flush. Canopy should be covered till the run off stage. (cid:120) Avoid the repeat application of particular pesticide and do not use expired pesticide. (cid:120) Prepare spray solution first in small quantity and then increase the volume to desired level by adding water. In case of wettable powder take required quantity of pesticide, add little quantity of water, mix it thoroughly to prepare the paste and then add remaining quantity of water to this paste with constant stirring. (cid:120) Avoid spraying during strong winds, cloudy days and drizzling. (cid:32)(cid:38)

Pests of Fruit Trees (Citrus, Banana, Mango, Pomegranate and Sapota) BANANA . Thangavelu, . . Shaikh, Suresh Pardeshi and .. Ahuja Major insect pests and diseases of banana and their management Major Insect Pests Thrips (Chaetanaphothrips signipennis) Symptoms (cid:120) Distinctive reddish brown oval stains on the finger, which can extend the entire length. (cid:120) In severe cases peel splits and the exposed flesh quickly discolors. Pest identification (cid:120) Egg: Kidney shaped, invisible to naked eye, laid just below the fruit or inside the pseudostem. (cid:120) Larva: They are wingless creamy white, smaller Rust thrips infestation on but have the same shape as the adult. fingers (cid:120) Pupa: Pupae are white like the larvae, can crawl, mm in length. (cid:120) Adult: They are slender, creamy yellow to golden brown with delicate feathery wings and . mm long. Their wings have dark, eye-like spots at the base and are fringed; when the wings are folded, the adult appears to have black line down its back. Biology of the pest Adult banana rust thrips reproduce sexually. After mating, females lay eggs in plant tissues where the thrips feed and hatch in – days. The newly hatched yellow nymphs feed for few days before moulting and after –10 days, mature (cid:32)(cid:39)

NCIPM nymphs move off the host plant into the soil and undergo pupation with the adult emerging in –10 days. The entire life cycle is completed in approximately 28 days. Management practices (cid:120) At the time of shooting, cover the bunch firmly (without any space between polythene cover and bunch) with 100 gauge thickness polythene sleeve having to 10% holes or polypropylene bunch leaves. (cid:120) Remove the male flower buds after opening of all hands. (cid:120) When the bud is in upright position, bud injection with imidacloprid 17.8EC @ 2ml chemical solution (. ml of chemical dissolved in 500 ml of water) per bud using disposable syringe. (cid:120) Spray the bunch with chlorpyriphos @ . ml/ litre of water/ acetamiprid 20SP (.0025 %) .. .25 / 10 liter of water along with sticking agent (@ .5ml/litre of water, two times . one at the time of opening of hands and second spray after all the hands opened. (cid:120) Verticillium lecanii ( 108 cfu/) 3g/ lit + 1ml sticker or NSKE % + sticker can be taken up as second spray. Aphids (Pentalonia nigronervosa) Symptoms (cid:120) Banana leaves are bunched into rosette appearance with leaf margins becoming wavy and upward rolling thereby reducing the growth and vigour of plant. Severly infected plants do not produce bunches and act as vector of bunchy top disease. (cid:120) Noticed in colonies on leaf axils and pseudostem. Pest identification (cid:120) Eggs: Egg stage not present and young ones are born live. (cid:120) Nymph: Oval or slightly elongated, reddish brown with six Aphid segmented antennae. infestation (cid:120) Adult: Reddish to dark brown/ almost black, shiny, small to medium sized. Biology of the pest It is phloem feeder that uses its long stylet to pierce plant tissues to suck the sap directly from the vessels. Reproduction is entirely parthenogenetic (without mating). Females give birth directly to live young female. The life cycle from nymph to adult is completed in to 16 days. (cid:32)(cid:40)

Pests of Fruit Trees (Citrus, Banana, Mango, Pomegranate and Sapota) Management practices (cid:120) Adopt clean cultivation using healthy and pest free suckers. (cid:120) Remove the affected plants and do not take up ratoon and inter crops. (cid:120) Drench the petioles, furled leaves, whorls or young suckers with soapy water or insecticidal soap. (cid:120) Spray dimethoate (75ml/100lit) or acephate (.3g/lit) on infested plants and suckers. (cid:120) Use of braconid wasps, Lysiphlebius testaceipes as parasitoid. (cid:120) Release predators such as lady bird beetles and lace wings @ insects/ plant. (cid:120) Apply bio control agent Beauveria bassiana ( ml in 100 ml water) between the leaf sheath at the base. Corm weevil (Cosmopolites sordidus) Symptoms (cid:120) The young grub tunnels into the base of suckers, roots and rhizome/ corm. (cid:120) Presence of larval tunnels on the entire length of corm rhizome. (cid:120) Yellowing and withering of leaves, reduced plant vigour, root Corm weevil () adult, () Grub destruction, reduced fruit production and are easily blown over by the wind. Pest identification (cid:120) Egg: White colour, elongate to oval, about to mm long and present on upper surface and crevices of rhizome. (cid:120) Grub: Legless grubs, creamy white with red head having strong mouthparts, stout and distinctly curved. (cid:120) Pupa: They are white and found inside the rhizome tunnels. (cid:120) Adult: . Weevils newly emerged are reddish brown which turns almost black after few days, hard-shelled having long curved snout and about 10 to 16 mm long. (cid:33)(cid:31)

NCIPM . They are active at night, may live up to two years but are very sensitive to dehydration and will die within 48 hours if kept in dry substrate. Biology of the pest Females lay eggs singly in small cavities at the base of pseudostem, in the upper part of the corm, in roots near the soil surface and at the end of cut stems (stumps). They hatch after to days and pupation takes place in holes bored by the grubs and adults emerge from the pupae to days after pupation. Management practices (cid:120) Practice clean cultivation with the suckers pruned periodically and infested clumps are removed and destroyed. (cid:120) Crop rotation with non host crops like paddy and sugarcane. (cid:120) Ensure proper fertilization and weed removal. (cid:120) Use of pheromone trap @ 16 traps /ha. (cid:120) Disc-on-stump traps can be used for trapping weevils. (cid:120) Application of bio control agents, Beauveria bassiana and Metarhizium anisopliae @ 3ml in 100 ml of water causes more than 90% mortality of the weevils. (cid:120) Application of carbofuran 3G @ 40g or rugby 10G @ 10g/plant neem cake @ 500 /plant at planting and then at three months after planting. (cid:120) Sucker dipping in triazophos solution (. ml in lit water) for about 20 minutes kills the eggs and grubs. (cid:120) Remove the entire plants after harvest and treat the pit with carbaryl (1g/ lit) after or chlorpyrifhos (.5ml/lit of water). Stem weevil / pseudostem borer (Odoiporus longicollis) Symptoms (cid:120) Presence of small pinhead-sized holes on the stem. (cid:120) Jelly exudation on the stem is the initial symptom of damage. (cid:120) Due to secondary infection of pathogens, rotting occurs and foul odour is emitted. (cid:120) After flowering, when tunnelling occurs in the true stem and peduncle, the fruits do not develop properly. Stem weevil (cid:33)(cid:32)

Pests of Fruit Trees (Citrus, Banana, Mango, Pomegranate and Sapota) Pest identification (cid:120) Egg: Creamy, cylindrical with rounded ends. (cid:120) Grub: They are fleshy, yellowish white dark brown head and apodous. (cid:120) Pupa: Pupate in fibrous cocoon, pale yellow colour and is exarate. (cid:120) Adult: Brownish black and measure 23-39 mm. Biology of the pest Gravid females lay eggs at random on cut ends of pseudostem.The incubation period ranges from to days. The larvae pass through five instars with the fifth instar larvae entering non-feeding pre-pupal stage and after 15 days of pupation, adult emerges. The total life cycle completes about in 55-60 days. Management practices (cid:120) After harvesting the bunch, remove the pseudostem from ground level and destroy them in order to avoid it serving as breeding site for the pest. (cid:120) Uproot and destroy infested plants. (cid:120) Use longitudinal pseudostem traps @ 100/ha for trapping weevils. (cid:120) Swabbing with chlorpyripos .5ml/ + adjuvant 1ml/ on the stem prevents infestation of stem weevil. (cid:120) When jelly exudation is noticed, inject 2ml triazophos solution (350 ml in 150 ml water). Two injections per plant at and feet above the ground level till flowering. The injection needle should enter only two or three leaf sheaths and should not touch the central core. Other Pest Burrowing nematode (Radopholus similis) Symptoms (cid:120) Reddish-brown to black, elongated discoloured area seen parallel to the root axis which eventually blacken and die. (cid:120) Lack of vigour in infested plants and poor fruiting observed. (cid:120) Infested plants are readily toppled Burrowing nematode [www.entnemdept.ufl.edu] and the roots get exposed. (cid:33)(cid:33)

NCIPM Biology of the pest The nematode is migratory endoparasite, which completes its life cycle in about 21 days at 25ºC in the root corm tissues. Females and juvenile stages both attack and enter host roots, especially near the tip of the roots. Males do not feed, as their stylets are weak. Females lay an average of - eggs each day. Management practices (cid:120) Crop rotation with non host crops. (cid:120) Adopt soil solarization. (cid:120) Remove all the black or discolored spots on the corm and root tissue, leaving only clean white tissues. (cid:120) Grow marigold as repellent and trap crop in the inter space. (cid:120) Use of neem cake @ 500g/ plant along with anyone of the bioagents Trichoderma viride/ Paecilomyces lilacinus/ Pseudomonas fluorescens @ 20 / plant. (cid:120) Application of carbofuran @ 50 / plant. (cid:120) Nematicide rugby 10G applied at 10g/ plant during 3rd and 5th month reduced the nematode population and increased the yield. Major Diseases Sigatoka leaf spot (Mycosphaerella eumusae) Symptoms (cid:120) Small, pale spots on leaves that turn into elliptical shaped dark purplish black spots with grey centres. (cid:120) Disease more prevalent on shallow, poorly drained soil. (cid:120) Severity of symptoms depicts burnt appearance of leaves. Leaf spot (cid:120) Poor filling and quality of bunches. (cid:120) Fruits don’ mature uniformly. Pathogen dissemination (cid:120) Wind, rain water and old dried infected leaves carrying conidiophores/ ascosphores help to spread the disease. (cid:33)(cid:34)

Pests of Fruit Trees (Citrus, Banana, Mango, Pomegranate and Sapota) Management practices (cid:120) The field must be kept weed free and clean. Follow either hand weeding/ harrowing till months after planting or use herbicide- glyphosate -10 ml/ litre of water + 25 of urea or ammonium sulphate/ tank or by intercropping with cowpea. (cid:120) While planting, optimum/ recommended spacing (. . ) must be followed. (cid:120) Provide adequate drainage facility whenever it is required. (cid:120) Apply only the recommended dose of fertilizer - , , / plant (200:40:400) as per the schedule + 25g azospirilium + 25 phosphorus solublizing bacteria. Application of Neem cake @ . to kg/plant may also be applied. Potash can be applied 10 to 20% more. Micronutrient mixture 10 /plant in 3rd month and 5th month after planting must be applied. (cid:120) Remove disease infected leaves or part of leaves & destroy it outside the orchard. (cid:120) No dried leaves should be hanging around the plant. (cid:120) The following pesticides may be applied as soon as the symptom appears on the leaves. The interval between two sprays may be 20 to 25 days. The fungicide requirement for litre of water is as follows. . Mineral oil 10 ml + propiconazole .% (1ml per litre of water). . Mineral oil 10 ml + carbendazim .% (1g per litre of water). . Mineral oil 10 ml + tridemorph .% (1g per litre of water). . Mineral oil 10 ml + companion (mancozeb and carbendazim) .% (1g per litre of water). . Mineral oil 10 ml + propiconazole .% (1ml per litre of water). . Mineral oil 10 ml + carbendazim .% (1g per litre of water). . Mineral oil 10 ml + tridemorph .% (1ml per litre of water). . Mineral oil 10 ml + propiconazole .% (1ml per litre of water). Note: . When oil (Banole–banana spray oil) is used no sticking agent is required. . Spray must be done in such way that the spray should cover ) both the sides of the leaves, and ) all the leaves including the top most unfurled leaf. . Only the recommended dose of chemicals should be applied to avoid development of resistance to fungicides, to reduce the cost of cultivation, to reduce the environmental pollution and also to avoid residue problems in the produce. (cid:33)(cid:35)

NCIPM Anthracnose (Colletotrichum musae) Symptoms (cid:120) Small, circular, black spots develop on the affected fruits. (cid:120) At the initial stage dark brown patches on immature fruits. (cid:120) Severe infestation leads to shriveled and black coloured rotten fruits covered with pink spore masses, which gradually spreads and affects the whole bunch. Anthracnose Pathogen dissemination (cid:120) Air-borne conidia and numerous insects visiting banana flowers spread the disease. (cid:120) High atmospheric temperature, humidity, wounds and bruises caused in the fruit favours disease development. Management practices (cid:120) Adopt clean cultivation and maintain proper field sanitation. (cid:120) When all the hands are opened, the distal bud should be removed to prevent infection. (cid:120) Transported bunches should be stored carefully at 14ºC without causing any bruises. (cid:120) Avoid contamination in collecting places, during transport and in ripening rooms. (cid:120) Preharvest spray with carbendazim .% two times at monthly interval. (cid:120) Postharvest dipping of fruits in carbendazim 400 ppm or benomyl 1000 ppm solution. Banana bract mosaic virus (BBMV) Symptoms (cid:120) Presence of spindle shaped pinkish to reddish streaks on pseudostem, petiole, midrib and peduncle. (cid:120) At emergence, suckers exhibit unusual reddish brown streaks and separation of leaf sheath from central axis. (cid:120) Crowding of leaves at crown which appears like palm leaf frill with elongated peduncle and half filled hands. BBMV (cid:33)(cid:36)

Pests of Fruit Trees (Citrus, Banana, Mango, Pomegranate and Sapota) Pathogen dissemination (cid:120) Disease spread is mainly through suckers planting materials. (cid:120) Aphid vectors such as Aphis gossypii, Pentalonia nigronervosa and Rhopalosiphum maidis also transmit the disease. Management practices (cid:120) Use disease free planting material. (cid:120) Avoid taking suckers for planting from the diseased plants. (cid:120) Practice clean cultivation and remove weeds which might harbour virus. (cid:120) Control of insect vector by spraying dimethoate (75 ml / 100 ) or acephate (. /). (cid:120) Apply more amount of organic manures especially cakes and 25% more of recommended fertilizers. Banana bunchy top virus (BBTV) Symptoms (cid:120) Yellowing of leaf margin initially and dark green streaks on the leaves. (cid:120) New leaves emerge with difficulty, are narrower than normal, wavy rather than flat, and have yellow (chlorotic) leaf margins. (cid:120) Leaves form bunch at the top. (cid:120) Usually fruiting doesn’ occur in severely infected banana plants but if produced, the banana hands and fingers are likely to be distorted and twisted. BBTV Pathogen dissemination (cid:120) It is transmitted by infected suckers and banana aphid. Management practices (cid:120) Use of virus free planting materials. (cid:120) Rouging and removal of infected banana plants. (cid:120) Practice clean cultivation. (cid:120) The diseased trees should be injected with ml of fernoxone solution (50g in 400 ml of water) or insertion of fernoxone capsules (containing 200 to 400 mg of chemical per capsule) into the pseudostem to kill the virus infected plants. (cid:33)(cid:37)

NCIPM (cid:120) Spray banana plants with dimethoate 30EC @ 1ml/ of water along with sticking agents to control insect vectors. Cigar end rot (Verticillium theobromae) Symptoms (cid:120) Black necrosis from the perianth to the tip of immature fruits causing shrinkage and folding of the tissues. (cid:120) Fingers that appear rotten are full of grey conidia, which look like the ash of cigar. Pathogen dissemination Cigar end rot (cid:120) Warm and moist conditions favour the disease occurrence and the disease spread is high in old and badly maintained plantations. Management practices (cid:120) Avoid overcrowding of plants ensuring enough aeration in plantations. (cid:120) Young bunches should be exposed to light and air and the stray bracts should be removed especially during wet weather. (cid:120) Improved sanitation helps in the reduction of the disease. (cid:120) Removal of pistil and perianth by hand immediately after the fruits are formed. Pistils should be removed to 11 days after bunch emergence. (cid:120) Spraying the bunches with carbendazim at .% after shoot emergence. Erwinia rot (Erwinia carotovora) Symptoms (cid:120) Affected young suckers show rotting and emits foul odour. (cid:120) The rot progresses up the pseudostem causing internal decay often with vascular discoloration. (cid:120) If affected plants are pulled, it comes out from the collar region leaving the corm with their Erwinia rot infected corm roots in the soil. (cid:120) Infection at late stage shows splitting of pseudostem in some cultivars. (cid:33)(cid:38)

Pests of Fruit Trees (Citrus, Banana, Mango, Pomegranate and Sapota) (cid:120) Yellowish to reddish ooze seen when affected plants are cut open at collar region. (cid:120) Soft rotting may spread radially towards growing point through the cortical tissues. Pathogen dissemination (cid:120) The pathogen being soil borne enters through wounds and also through leaf sheath of suckers. (cid:120) Hot and damp weather with plenty of rainfall trigger the disease development. (cid:120) Water is required for the bacteria to invade into the plant. Management practices (cid:120) Use disease free suckers for planting. (cid:120) Removal and destruction of infected plants. Rainy season planting should be avoided. (cid:120) Grow sunnhemp or cowpea as intercrop to provide shade to the banana plants. (cid:120) Give regular irrigation only in either morning or evening hrs. (cid:120) Apply the plants with bleaching powder @ 8g/plant at 10 to 15 days interval for - times. (cid:120) Use healthy planting material. (cid:120) Drench plants with the solution of 300g copper oxychloride + 15g streptocycline + 300ml chlorpyriphos in liter of water. Panama wilt (Fusarium oxysporum . sp. cubense) Symptoms (cid:120) Visual observations show yellowing of lower leaves, longitudinal splitting of pseudostem and wilting of plants. (cid:120) Advanced stages of disease shows plants with spiky appearance due to prominent upright apical leaves. (cid:120) The xylem (water conducting) vessels turn reddish- brown as the fungus grows through the tissues. Fusarium wilt infected plant (cid:33)(cid:39)

NCIPM Pathogen dissemination (cid:120) It is soil borne disease and the fungus enters the roots through the fine laterals. (cid:120) Infected rhizomes or suckers, soil adhering to farm implements, irrigation water is responsible for the spread of the fungus. Management practices Fusarium wilt infected corm (cid:120) Follow proper crop rotation. (cid:120) Grow wilt resistant cultivars in endemic areas and avoid susceptible varieties. (cid:120) Use pathogen free suckers and dip in % carbendazim before planting. (cid:120) Use tissue cultured plants. (cid:120) Practice clean cultivation with proper fertilization, irrigation and weed control. (cid:120) Provide good drainage especially during rainy season and use organic soil amendments. (cid:120) Apply neem cake @ 250 Kg/ha. (cid:120) Application of bioagent Trichoderma viride and Pseudomonas fluorescens along with farmyard manure and neem cake. (cid:120) Drenching of carbendazim . % solution @ - liter/plant in the soil around the plants - times at monthly interval starting from 1st month of planting. (cid:33)(cid:40)

Pests of Fruit Trees (Citrus, Banana, Mango, Pomegranate and Sapota) MANGO . . Godse, Pushpa . Patil, Ajay Munj and . . Ahuja Major insect pests and diseases of mango and their manangement Major Insect Pests Mango hopper (Idioscopus clypealis, .nitidulus and Amritodus atkinsoni) It is most serious of all the mango pests and prevalent all over the country causing heavy damage to mango crop. Though hopper population exists throughout the year in mango orchards but occasionally it advances during January to April on flowering flush. Also noticed during June-August on vegetative flush. Old, neglected and closely planted orchards that are shady and with high humid conditions favour their multiplication. Symptoms (cid:120) Piercing and sap sucking of tender parts by nymphs and adults causing reduction of vigour that leads to shedding of flower buds, flowers and young fruits. (cid:120) Development of sooty mould due to honey dew secretion on leaves gives blackish appearance. (cid:120) Hoppers hibernate in the crevices of the barks on the tree. (cid:120) During higher infestation periods, characteristic clicking sounds of leaf hoppers can be heard. (cid:120) Warm, humid and cloudy climate is most congenial for insect development. Mango hopper (cid:34)(cid:31)

(cid:120) Adult – Golden brown or dark brown resembling to bark colour wedge- shaped. Size: – mm in length. . Idioscopus niveosparsus: dark with wavy lines on wings and three spots on scutellum. . Idioscopus clypealis: small, light brown with dark spots on the vertex and two spots on scutellum. . Amirtodus atkinsoni: large, light brown with two spots on scutellum. Biology of pest Leafhoppers will breed all year round if tender flush is available. The female hoppers insert 100-200 eggs on mid rib of tender leaves, buds and inflorescence. Eggs hatch in two to three days and nymphs develop between 12 to 20 days. The nymphs develop faster during the flowering and fruiting period. The total life cycle occupies - weeks. They complete - generations in flowering period itself. Management practices (cid:120) Avoid dense planting, maintain clean orchards, prune overlapping branches and infested shoots. (cid:120) Neem based sprays can be utilized at initial stage of hopper population (Azadirachtin 3000 ppm@2ml/). Tetrastichus sp. and fungus, Verticillium lecanii. Second at panicle initiation stage with quinalphos 25% 20 ml or carbaryl 50% 20 gm or profenophos 50% 10 ml per 10 lit. of water. Subsequent sprays with imidachloprid 17.% ml or clothianidin 50% (WDG) . gm or thiamethoxam 25% (WDG) gm or triazophos 40% 10 ml or phenthoate 50% 20 ml or diamethoate 30% 10 ml or deltamethrin % + triazophos 35% 10 ml or Lambda cyhalothrin % ml per 10 lit. of water. Pests of Fruit Trees (Citrus, Banana, Mango, Pomegranate and Sapota) Mealybug (Drosicha mangiferae) In India, it is widely distributed along the Indo-gangetic plains and found in Punjab, Uttar Pradesh, Bihar and Delhi causes severe damage to mango crop. It attacks almost all the plant parts. Symptoms (cid:120) Pinkish nymphs and adult mealy bugs are present on leaves, inflorescence, branches, fruits and fruit stalk. (cid:120) The nymphs of this pest suck sap from leaves and inflorescence causing dryness leading to flower drop and negligible fruit set. (cid:120) They also secrete honey dew which gives rise Mango mealybug to sooty mold attack. Pest identification (cid:120) Nymph – They are flat in shape and pink to brown in colour. (cid:120) Adult – The adult male is small and winged while the female is bigger and wingless. The females can be identified by their flat shape, covered with white flocculent wax covering. Biology of pest They complete one generation each year. Females mate and crawl down the tree during the month of April-May and lays egg in the soil in large numbers sheathed in an egg sac. The eggs lie in diapause state in the soil till the return of the favourable conditions in the month of November - December. First instars move to the leaves and moult three times to become adults. Just after hatching the nymphs crawl up the tree. They are considered more important because of their activeness and infestation during the flowering season. Management practices (cid:120) Proper orchard maintenance by removal of weeds that harbor mealy bugs. (cid:120) Ploughing of the orchard during November-December. (cid:120) Flooding of orchard with water and raking of soil around tree trunk exposes the eggs to sun and natural enemies thereby destroying them. (cid:120) Banding of tree trunk with polythene sheets (400 gauge) 30 cm above ground level and just below the junction of branching to obstruct the ascent of the nymphs. Banding should be done well in advance before the hatching of eggs, .., around November - December. (cid:34)(cid:33)

(cid:120) Application of methyl parathion dust % @ 250 per tree in the soil around the trunk during 3rd or 4th week of December. (cid:120) Early instar nymphs of the mealy bug can be controlled by spraying of .05 % carbaryl from January to March. Leaf webber (Orthaga euadrusalis) It is pest that is attaining serious proportions mainly in North India especially in old, crowded orchards where there is excessive shade. Pest infestation begins from the month of April and continues up to December. The species Orthaga exvinacea is found commonly throughout the plains of South India. Symptoms (cid:120) Infestations of leaf webber may begin as early as seedling stage and persist even during flowering and fruiting. (cid:120) Webbing of terminal leaves and tender shoots with several caterpillars found inside. (cid:120) Caterpillars initially scrap and feed on the terminal leaves within the web and give burnt Leaf webber damage appearance to leaves. Pest identification (cid:120) Larva - Pale greenish with brown head and prothoracic shield. (cid:120) Adult - Brown moth with wavy lines on fore wings. Biology of pest (cid:120) The life-cycle completes in - weeks. Eggs are laid in clusters of about ten on buds and young leaves. After hatching the larvae forms web around the leaflets and feed on the tender leaves. Pupation takes place inside the webs in silken cocoons. The last generation pupates in the soil around December-January. Management practices Adult, pupa & larvae of leaf (cid:120) Webbed leaves should be removed webber [courtesy internet] (cid:34)(cid:34)

Stem borer (Batocera rufomaculata) This pest is widely distributed in India and attacks not only mango but also other varieties of fruits. Symptoms (cid:120) Grubs feed inside the stem boring upward making irregular tunnels which results in interruption of nutrient and water transport in the tissue. Scale insect (Chloropulvinaria polygonata, Aspidiotus destructor) In India mango is attacked by several species of scale insects, the predominant ones being Chloropulvinaria polygonata, Aspidiotus destructor, Parlatoria pergandii, .cinerea and Lepidosaphes gloverii. Shoot gall psylla (Apsylla cistellata) It is serious pest of North India and particularly reported from Uttar Pradesh, Bihar and Terrai regions of northern India. The pest is active from August onwards with the nymphs emerging from eggs during August-September and crawling to the adjacent buds to suck cell sap. As result of feeding, the buds develop into hard conical green galls which are usually seen during September-October. Symptoms (cid:120) Terminal shoots affected. Fruit fly (Bactrocera dorsalis) In India, eight species of genus Bactrocera are identified among quarantine pests with the oriental fruit fly . dorsalis Hendel being the most destructive fruit fly of mango, followed by Peach fruit fly . zonata Saunders and Guava fruit fly . correcta Bezzi. Mango malformation (Fusarium spp.) Mango malformation is serious disease in tropical and subtropical areas of the world and has been attributed to various Fusarium spp. Mango malformation disease is caused by one or more species of the fungus Fusarium. The disease spreads on tree very slowly, but if left unchecked, can reduce yields in orchards. Mango is the only known host of the disease. It is result of hormonal imbalance in the trees, induced by the Fusarium infections and associated with bud mite (Aceria mangiferae) infestations. Symptoms (cid:120) Deformation of vegetative and floral tissues. (cid:120) Growing tips produce distorted shoots with Mango vegetative malformation short internodes and affected leaves smaller than normal leaves giving compact and bunchy-top appearance while the flowers are sterile and don’ bear fruits. (cid:120) Both normal and malformed growth can be present on the plant at the same time. Pathogen dissemination Mango floral malformation The fungus, Fusarium mangiferae produces both macro and micro spores which are the infective propagules. The main spread of mango malformation disease to new areas is by infected pruning equipment or grafted planting material. The bud mite, Aceria mangiferae is also shown to spread the disease within tree and not between trees. Management practices (cid:120) Orchards may be kept in good hygienic conditions and disease free planting material be used for planting. (cid:120) Regular pruning of malformed panicles and parts along with affected shoots. (cid:120) Simple, effective and eco-friendly control can be attained by using leaf extracts of neem tree and common weeds namely Datura stramonium, Calotropis gigantea etc. (cid:35)(cid:38)

Dieback/gummosis (Lasiodiplodia theobromae) The disease is observed all year round but is most evident during June, July and August and low during cool months from November to February. Disease is accompanied by damage caused by trunk borers resembling Batocera reformaculata. It is common soil-borne saprophyte or wound parasite, distributed throughout the tropics and subtropics. Symptoms (cid:120) Characteristic leaf drooping and drying that leads to defoliation lending scorched look to the tree. (cid:120) Trees exude gummy stuff from the bark of their trunks or branches and vascular tissues are discolored. Management practices (cid:120) Use of disease free propagating material. (cid:120) Pruning and disinfection of infected branches. (cid:120) Proper disposal and burning of affected branches. (cid:120) Two foliar sprays with topsin- (thiphanate-methyl) @ / or foliar spray with carbendazim @ .%, or chlorothalonil @ .% at fortnightly interval. (cid:120) Dolomite kg/plant is recommended. Kulkarni Major insect pests and diseases of pomegranate and their management Major Insect Pests Pomegranate fruit borer (Deudorix isocrates) Pomegranate fruit borer is distributed all over India and Asia. It is the most widespread, polyphagous and destructive pest of pomegranate. The damage of fruit borer is seen throughout the year irrespective of the bahar. Symptoms (cid:120) The female butterfly lays eggs on flowers, buds and the calyx of developing fruits. After hatching, caterpillars enter the fruit and feed on the arils. (cid:120) The striking symptom is the odious smell and excreta of caterpillars coming out of the entry holes ultimately leading to fruit rot. Fruit borer Adult Larva Pupa Bored hole on fruit with excreta Pest identification (cid:120) Egg: They are laid singly on tender leaves, stalks and flower buds. (cid:120) Larva: Full-grown larvae are dark brown with short hair and white patches all over the body and measures about 16 to 20mm long. (cid:36)(cid:37)

NCIPM (cid:120) Pupa: Pupation occurs either inside the damaged fruits or on the stalk holding it. (cid:120) Adult: Adults are glossy bluish in the case of male and brownish violet in the case of female with conspicuous orange patch on the forewings. Biology of the pest Adults lay eggs on the stalks or flower buds with incubation period lasting -10 days. The larva hatches and bores into the fruit with the larval period lasting for 18-47 days. Pupation lasts for -34 days and the life cycle is completed in - months. Management practices (cid:120) Remove and destroy the affected fruits. (cid:120) Clip off calyx cup immediately after pollination followed by two applications of neem oil @ %. (cid:120) Before maturity, bag the fruits with butter paper. (cid:120) At flowering stage, spray NSKE % or neem formulations ml/. (cid:120) Spray deltamethrin . EC (. ml/litre of water) at fortnightly interval from the stage of flowering to fruit development. (cid:120) Spray malathion 50 EC .% or methomyl 40 SP @ . ml/ or azadirachtin 1500 ppm @ . ml/ at 15 days intervals commencing from initiation of flowering up to the harvesting subjected to the presence of fruit borer. It is polyphagous pest of minor importance boring the stems and trunk of pomegranate trees. It prefers breeding in dead wood but also attacks the living branches. The grubs of stem borer bore into the cambium then girdle the stem or branch causing death of the tree. Symptoms (cid:120) Holes made by grubs are seen on bark of main stems. Grubs feed internally on sapwood while adult beetles are active by the day and feed by gnawing the green bark of shoots. (cid:120) Usually, excreta and dry powdered material is seen near the base of plants. (cid:36)(cid:39)
